moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the most classical of all chess moves, immediately claiming the center and freeing both our bishop and queen for future development. This move controls the important d5 and f5 squares, giving us excellent central influence right from the start. Our pawn on e4 forms the foundation of many aggressive attacking systems, and we're inviting Black to meet us in the center with moves like e5 or c5. The king's pawn opening leads to sharp, tactical games where both sides fight for the initiative from move one.

Our opponent chooses the provocative Alekhine Defense, immediately attacking our central pawn and inviting us to advance aggressively. This hypermodern approach aims to provoke our pawns forward where they might become targets later in the game. Black is essentially saying 'go ahead, chase my knight and overextend your center - I'll use your advanced pawns as targets.' The Alekhine is a sharp, uncompromising defense that leads to complex middlegame positions where both sides must play precisely. While less popular than 1...e5 or 1...c5, it's a legitimate weapon that can catch unprepared opponents off guard.

We're accepting Black's challenge by advancing our pawn and attacking the knight, gaining space in the center and forcing our opponent's pieces to retreat. This natural advance gives us excellent central control and opens up the f1-a6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop. Our pawn on e5 restricts Black's pieces and gives us a space advantage that we'll try to convert into a kingside attack. The key idea is that while we're advancing pawns, we're also gaining time by forcing Black's knight to move repeatedly, allowing us to build up our position with tempo.

Black retreats the knight to the excellent central square d5, where it eyes several important squares and maintains flexibility for future moves. From d5, the knight can jump to various squares like f4, e7, b6, or c7 depending on how the position develops. Our opponent is following the main line of the Alekhine Defense, keeping the knight actively placed rather than retreating it to the rim with moves like Nh5 or Ng8. This centralized knight will need to be dealt with soon, as it's well-placed and could become annoying if we allow it to stay there permanently.

We're building a massive pawn center with this advance, gaining even more space and supporting our advanced e5 pawn. This move opens the c1-h6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop and prepares to kick the centralized knight with c4. Our pawn duo on d4 and e5 gives us excellent central control and restricts Black's pieces significantly. The Four Pawns Attack gets its name from our plan to place pawns on c4, d4, e5, and f4, creating maximum central pressure. While this looks aggressive and promising, we must be careful not to overextend - these advanced pawns can also become weaknesses if we're not precise.

Our opponent strikes back at our pawn chain, targeting the head of our pawn formation on e5. This is Black's most principled response, as it challenges our central dominance and opens the c8-bishop's diagonal. By attacking e5, Black is following the classic principle of striking at the base or head of a pawn chain rather than attacking the middle. This move also prepares potential piece development and gives Black some central counterplay. We'll need to decide whether to advance with exd6, maintain the tension, or support our e5 pawn - each choice leads to different types of positions.

We're launching our fourth pawn into the center, attacking the knight and completing our aggressive pawn formation. This move forces Black's knight to retreat again, giving us another tempo while expanding our space advantage even further. Our pawn on c4 controls important squares like b5 and d5, and works together with our d4 pawn to dominate the center. The Four Pawns Attack is now taking shape - we have maximum central control but must be prepared for Black's counterattacks against our extended pawn structure. This aggressive setup often leads to sharp tactical battles where precise calculation becomes crucial.

Black's knight retreats to b6, where it attacks our c4 pawn and eyes the excellent d5 square for potential future occupation. From b6, the knight maintains some activity while staying out of immediate danger from our advancing pawns. Our opponent is following sound positional principles by keeping the knight active rather than allowing it to be pushed to the rim. This retreat also prepares Black's queenside development and maintains pressure on our pawn structure. The knight on b6 will be watching for opportunities to jump back into the center, particularly to d5 if we're not careful with our piece coordination.

We're completing our massive four-pawn center, gaining maximum space and preparing a potential kingside attack. This advance opens the f1-a6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop and supports a future advance of our kingside pawns. Our pawn on f4 controls the important e5 and g5 squares, and works with our other pawns to create a formidable central wall. The Four Pawns Attack is now fully deployed, giving us excellent attacking chances but also creating potential weaknesses that Black will try to exploit. This aggressive pawn structure often leads to opposite-side castling and sharp tactical melees.

Our opponent chooses the Fianchetto Variation, preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop to g7 where it will eye our central pawns and the long diagonal. This setup is one of Black's most solid responses to our aggressive pawn advance, as the fianchettoed bishop will put pressure on our extended center. Black's plan involves castling kingside behind the fianchettoed bishop structure, then launching counterplay against our overextended pawns. The move g6 also prepares potential pawn breaks with f6 or h5, giving Black multiple ways to challenge our position. We must now decide how to complete our development while maintaining the initiative - typical plans include Be2, Nf3, and castling, followed by a kingside attack.
